text: David asked, "Is anyone still left from Saul's household? I want to show kindness to him for Jonathan's sake."
There was a man who had served in Saul's house named Ziba, and he was brought before David. "Are you Ziba?" the king asked. "I am your servant," he replied.
The king said, "Is there truly no one left of Saul's family to whom I might show God's own kindness?" Ziba answered, "Jonathan still has a son living; both his feet are crippled."
"Where is he?" the king asked. Ziba said, "He is staying at Lo-debar, in the household of Machir son of Ammiel."
So King David sent for him and had him brought from Machir's house at Lo-debar.
When Mephibosheth, son of Jonathan and grandson of Saul, reached David, he bowed low with his face to the ground. "Mephibosheth," said David, and he answered, "Here is your servant."

"Don't be afraid," David told him. "For your father Jonathan's sake I will be kind to you — I will give back to you all the property that belonged to your grandfather Saul, and from now on you will always share my table."
Bowing again, Mephibosheth said, "Who is your servant, that you should notice a dead dog like me?"
Then the king summoned Ziba, who had served Saul, and told him, "Everything that belonged to Saul and his family I am giving to your master's grandson.

You and your sons and your servants are to farm the land for him and bring in the harvest, so your master's grandson always has food. But Mephibosheth himself will always eat at my own table." (Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.)
Ziba said to the king, "Your servant will do exactly as my lord the king commands." So Mephibosheth ate at David's table like one of the king's own sons.
Mephibosheth had a small son named Mica, and everyone living in Ziba's household became servants to Mephibosheth.
So Mephibosheth made his home in Jerusalem, since he always ate at the king's table — though he remained lame in both feet.
